narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid middle-class family background, your law school application stood out in some ways but ultimately didn't make the cut due to the admissions model's assessment of your bar exam prospects. The model, which looks at patterns from thousands of past students' academic records, family details, and test scores to predict first-time bar exam success, calculated a 45.4% probability that you would fail the exam—high enough to trigger a rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass on their first try. Your LSAT score of 38, which is above the typical 36.7 average, was the single strongest factor working in your favor, strongly nudging the prediction toward expecting you to pass by showing your aptitude for the kind of reasoning the bar demands. However, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1—well below the average of around 5.5—emerged as the most influential drag, signaling to the model that your later undergraduate performance placed you in the bottom tier compared to peers, which heavily pushed toward a failure prediction. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.8, lower than the 3.21 average, also played a key role against you, though less intensely than your decile, as it reinforced concerns about consistent academic strength relative to typical successful bar takers. Being a full-time student, like over 90% of applicants, was neutral and didn't sway things much. Overall, while your test score gave you an edge over many applicants, the model saw your weaker decile and GPA as bigger red flags, placing your profile among those at higher risk compared to the stronger academic trajectories of admitted students. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a lawyer—it's just the model's data-driven snapshot. To turn things around, consider retaking the LSAT to push even higher or bolstering your later academic record through a post-bac program or additional coursework that could lift your decile equivalent. Building a narrative around any upward trends or extenuating circumstances in your application essays might also help for other schools. Many students in similar spots have succeeded by targeting programs with holistic reviews or those valuing LSAT strength over GPA. Keep pushing—you've got strengths to build on.
